Summary: Earlier releases of MATLAB crash when simulating a model with MATLAB function blocks after simulating in R2011a
Description: Simulating a model containing MATLAB function blocks using R2011a creates a MEX file that is incompatible with prior releases of MATLAB. If you subsequently simulate this model from the same working directory using a version of MATLAB earlier than R2011a, MATLAB will crash. The generated stack trace begins with something similar to: [  0] 0x000007fed981e547 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/stateflow.dll+02155847 ( TagVisitorTraverserPreSF::operator=+126471 )[  1] 0x000007fed98216b5 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/stateflow.dll+02168501 ( TagVisitorTraverserPreSF::operator=+139125 )[  2] 0x000007fed9822279 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/stateflow.dll+02171513 ( TagVisitorTraverserPreSF::operator=+142137 )[  3] 0x000007fed98ad542 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/stateflow.dll+02741570 ( sfdd::StackAllocatedString::~StackAllocatedString+207890 )[  4] 0x000007fed98d9b18 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/stateflow.dll+02923288 ( SF::passthrough::SFLockNotifierexporter::operator=+123944 )[  5] 0x000007fed98d9b52 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/stateflow.dll+02923346 ( sf_mexFunction+000018 )[  6] 0x00000000699e3333 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/libmex.dll+00013107 ( mexRunMexFile+000131 )[  7] 0x00000000699e205f C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/libmex.dll+00008287 ( inSwapMexfileReader+000223 )[  8] 0x00000000699e2228 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/libmex.dll+00008744 ( inSwapMexfileReader+000680 )[  9] 0x0000000074b8f859 C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/m_dispatcher.dll+00063577 ( Mfh_file::dispatch_fh+000473 )[ 10] 0x0000000074b8fccf C:/Program Files/MATLAB/R2011a/bin/win64/m_dispatcher.dll+00064719 ( Mfunction_handle::dispatch+000767 )
Workaround: Use different working directories when using the same model with multiple versions of MATLAB.

Summary: startForeground command is slow to execute
Description: If you run a session with an NI-9211 module in a CompactDAQ 9171 chassis and use the default session rate and duration, the operation takes a long time to complete.

Summary: int returns incorrect results involving besselk
Description: For integrands of the form exp(z*cosh(x))*sinh(x)^a,  int  can return an incorrect result: syms v positive;syms t real;syms z positive;int(exp(-z*cosh(t))*sinh(t)^(2*v),t,0,inf)ans = (gamma(v + 1/2)*besselk(2*v, z))/(pi^(1/2)*(-z/2)^v) The correct result has besselk(v, z)  instead of besselk(2*v, z) . The following releases are affected: R2010a, R2010b, and R2011a.

Summary: Creating a new item in the Current Folder browser can rename an existing item
Description: Creating a new file or folder automatically opens a text box to rename the newly added item. If you have sorted the Current Folder Browser by Date Modified, the new name might be applied to a different item and not the newly created item.
Workaround: Sort items in the Current Folder browser by Name, Type or Size, and then create a new file or folder.

Summary: sldvmergeharness output model has incorrect simulation stop time
Description: The output model generated by sldvmergeharness  has an incorrect value for simulation stop time that matches the value of the simulation stop time in the first input model. The correct simulation stop time for the sldvmergeharness  output model is the largest simulation stop time out of all the input model values. The simulation stop time can be viewed on the Configuration Parameters dialog box in the Solver > Simulation time  pane.

Summary: sldvmergeharness output model unable to simulate when input models are configured to define parameter values
Description: The function sldvmergeharness  combines test data from two or more test harness models and outputs a new test harness model. When you run sldvmergeharness  on any input models that are configured to define parameter values other than the Simulink Design Verifier defaults, the output model will not be able to simulate without throwing an error.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier Test Generation analysis might end after 300 seconds and still show Undecided Test Objectives
Description: Simulink Design Verifier analysis might time out erroneously, ending before the Maximum analysis time has elapsed even though Test Objectives with Undecided status still exist.

Summary: Sample time mismatch error might occur when multiple asynchronous function calls drive one referenced model
Description: If a model has multiple asynchronous function calls that drive one referenced model, updating the model might generate an error similar to the following: Number of functional sample times specified in block 'topModel/TmpSFcnForModelReference_Model'does not match the number of sample times in this block. This can happen when you build anS-Function block from a variable-step model, and then use the S-Function block in afixed-step model. You can fix this error by switching to a variable-step solver orregenerating the S-Function block with a fixed-step solver. The error message also might be displayed if a model contains an S-function block that initiates multiple asynchronous function calls, and you make a change that affects sorting order, such as changing block names, adding or removing blocks, rewiring signals, and so on.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier report has invalid expected output values for blocks with multiple outports.
Description: The Simulink Design Verifier report has invalid expected output values for blocks with multiple outports.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier Design Error Detection fails, reports "Error using horzcat" in analysis of Function-Call Subsystems
Description: Simulink Design Verifier Design Error Detection fails during analysis of Function-Call Subsystems, throwing the following error: Error using horzcat CAT arguments dimensions are not consistent.  ?

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier Built-In Block Replacements fail for Switch and Multiport Switch blocks with Integer rounding mode set to Simplest
Description: Simulink Design Verifier built-in block replacement rules?for Switch and Multiport Switch blocks fail, generating errors, when the Integer rounding mode parameter of a Switch or Multiport Switch block is set to Simplest . The Integer rounding mode parameter selection is located in the Signal Attributes tab of the Function Block Parameters.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier Report might have invalid hyperlinks to replaced blocks if multiple block replacement rules exist
Description: In the generated Simulink Design Verifier Report for your model, the Block Replacements Summary might contain invalid hyperlinks to the replaced blocks if you specified multiple block replacement rules.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier analysis generates error when Model blocks reference models with From/Goto blocks that have matching tags
Description: If you run Simulink Design Verifier analysis on a model that contains a Model block, the error message "Invalid Simulink object name " appears when: The parent model and the referenced model contain From/Goto blocks with matching tags. The referenced model contains From/Goto blocks with global tag visibility. The From/Goto blocks in the referenced model are inside of a Subsystem block whose name ends in the name of the referenced model.  The parent model is the top-level model. The referenced model is inside the Model block.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier compatibility checking fails for models containing deprecated Lookup Table blocks and Mux blocks creating bus signals
Description: Simulink Design Verifier reports that checking compatibility failed due to multiple errors if a model includes both of the following: Deprecated Lookup Table blocks (blocks that no longer appear in the Lookup Tables Library). Mux blocks creating bus signals.

Summary: Unnecessary copy of parameters to block output in the generated code of the Outputs function
Description: It is possible that unneeded copies are in the generated code for both the Start  and Outputs  system functions. xPC targets are affected. The inefficient code might appear as: aModel_B.Counter = aModel_P.Counter_Value; ... if (aModel_BCounter < 2) {... when previously, it was: if (aModel_P.Counter < 2) {... ? ? ? ?
Workaround: There is no known work-around at this time.

Summary: Number of successfully analyzed Simulink Design Verifier Test Generation objectives might decrease compared to number from previous release
Description: In Test Generation analysis, the number of objectives satisfied or proven false by Simulink Design Verifier might decrease compared to the number successfully analyzed in a previous release. This occurs if your model contains counters or timers, since these tend to involve time delays. If other states are changing while counters are updating, Simulink Design Verifier Test Generation analysis might report a much lower number of objectives satisfied or proven false than it did in a previous release.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Instructions for Applying WorkaroundAfter installing the .zip file, restart MATLAB. Make sure that toolbox path caching is not enabled in the File > Preferences > General > Toolbox path caching pane.Reopen your model and run Simulink Design Verifier Test Generation on your model again.Locate the output directory of the Simulink Design Verifier analysis (sldv_output) and load the .mat data file that was generated by the analysis in step 2.Make sure that the MATLAB structure sldvData exists in the MATLAB workspace.Execute sldvtimer(sldvData) to understand whether timer optimization are applied during analysis.If sldvtimer(sldvData) returns 1, then disable timer optimizations by executing sldvtimer(0) and restart Simulink Design Verifier Test Generation on the model.You can get more information about sldvtimer by typing help sldvtimer.?Change log:September 14, 2011: attachment_756367_11b_win64_2011-09-14.zip: initial version, works on win64 R2011b onlySeptember 14, 2011: attachment_756367_11b_win32_2011-09-14.zip: initial version, works on win32 R2011b onlySeptember 14, 2011: attachment_756367_11b_glnxa64_2011-09-14.zip: initial version, works on glnxa64 R2011b onlySeptember 14, 2011: attachment_756367_11b_glnx86_2011-09-14.zip: initial version, works on glnx86 R2011b only??

Summary: Nonexistent or unexpected signal bounds/ranges might be derived in Simulink Design Verifier Design Error Detection analysis
Description: Simulink Design Verifier's Design Error Detection analysis might either not derive signal bounds or ranges, or might derive unexpected signal bounds or ranges, for signals passing through virtual blocks.

Summary: Interacting with Bus Selector dialog box can fail with the error message "getBlockHandlesData at 274 Assertion Failed"
Description: If the bus signal hierarchy definition is ambiguous, due to a loop involving the first non-control port of a Switch or Multiport Switch block, then the Bus Selector block connected to this bus signal may fail to update its dialog box.
Workaround: Modify your model so that the loop passes through one of the other inputs of the Switch or Multiport Switch block.

Summary: Overflow errors during compatibility check
Description: Simulink Design Verifier generates errors on overflow conditions even when model parameter IntegerOverflowMsg  is disabled
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier incorrectly reports unreachable test objectives in Stateflow charts as Undecided.
Description: If the model includes a Stateflow chart with a graphical function that has model coverage objectives, and that graphical function is not called anywhere in the model, then Simulink Design Verifier Test Generation mode incorrectly reports those objectives as Undecided. Since the graphical function is not called, Simulink Design Verifier should mark all of the model coverage objectives associated with it as Proven Unsatisfiable.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier compatibility checking fails if inactive subsystem of Variant Subsystem block contains Model block
Description: If an inactive subsystem of a Variant Subsystem block contains a Model block, Simulink Design Verifier compatibility checking fails and reports errors.

Summary: Invalid data type incompatibility error may be reported when using a MATLAB structure to initialize a bus signal
Description: Initializing a bus signal using a MATLAB structure can produce an invalid error about data type incompatibility, if one or more of the signals in this bus has an enumerated data type.

Summary: No adaptors are found on Ubuntu 11.04
Description: When the toolbox is used on the Ubuntu 11.04 platform, you may find that there are no adaptors found. For example, imaqhwinfo may show: >> imaqhwinfoans =InstalledAdaptors: {}  MATLABVersion: '7.13 (R2011b Prerelease)'  ToolboxName: 'Image Acquisition Toolbox'  ToolboxVersion: '4.2 (R2011b Prerelease)' ?
Workaround: To work around this, start by running the attached MATLAB function:>> findAdaptorsWithMissingDependenciesIt will let you know if an adaptor is missing a dependency. If this is not an adaptor you plan to use, you should rename it.For example, if it reports that mwdcamimaq.so is missing libraw1394.so.8 and you do not intend to use the dcam adaptor, you could rename mwdcamimaq.so to mwdcamimaq.so.orig, which will prevent the attempt to load the adaptor while preserving the file for future use. If instead you did intend to use the dcam adaptor, then you would want to resolve the missing dependency on libraw1394.so.8 by installing the correct version of libraw1394 on your system.To rename the adaptor files, the adaptor files can be found in:<MATLAB>/toolbox/imaq/imaqadaptors/<arch>?

Summary: Existing test cases are ignored when using the CombinedObjectives (Nonlinear Extended) strategy.
Description: Existing test cases provided by the user are ignored when generating test cases using the CombinedObjectives (Nonlinear Extended) strategy. CombinedObjectives (Nonlinear Extended) is a Test suite optimization  configuration parameter option.

Summary: MATLAB Function block documentation points to wrong path and names for Kalman tutorial files
Description: In the tutorial on Integrating MATLAB Code with a Simulink Model for Tracking a Moving Object,  Simulink documentation points to the wrong path for copying Kalman filter example files. The correct location of the files is: docroot\toolbox\simulink\examples\kalman . The references in the Simulink documentation are: web([docroot '/toolbox/simulink/ug/bszo62g.html#bslqab6-3'])  in the product help https://www.mathworks.com/help/releases/R2011b/toolbox/simulink/ug/bszo62g.html  in the online help as of 9/1/2011  The documentation also refers to the Simulink example model files by the wrong names. The correct names are: ex_kalman00.mdlex_kalman11.mdlex_kalman22.mdlex_kalman33.mdlex_kalman44.mdl The references in the Simulink documentation are: web([docroot '/toolbox/simulink/ug/bszo62g.html#bslqab6-4'])  in the product help https://www.mathworks.com/help/releases/R2011b/toolbox/simulink/ug/bszo62g.html#bslqab6-4  in the online help as of 9/1/2011
Workaround: Copy the following tutorial files from docroot\toolbox\simulink\examples\kalman:ex_kalman01.mex_kalman02.mex_kalman03.mex_kalman04.mex_kalman00.mdlex_kalman11.mdlex_kalman22.mdlex_kalman33.mdlex_kalman44.mdlposition.matplot_trajectory.m?

Summary: Intermittent data loss when you use VNT CAN Simulink blocks with NI hardware
Description: You can lose some CAN Messages when you simultaneously use CAN Receive and Transmit blocks to communicate with the same NI device and channel. This occurs in Vehicle Network Toolbox Version R2011a only.
Workaround: Please contact MathWorks Technical Support for a workaround.

Summary: Uncompilable code generated for parameter with ImportedExternPointer storage class
Description: If a model references a Simulink.Parameter  workspace variable, which has its storage class set to ImportedExternPointer , Simulink Coder might generate uncompilable C code.  The uncompilable code might appear when generating code for an atomic subsystem which has the Function packaging  parameter set to Reusable function  and the atomic subsystem references the workspace variable in a mask. In this case, the uncompilable code appears on the function call site of the outputs function generated for the atomic subsystem.

Summary: Polyspace verification options visible through Model Link SL GUI are not used
Description: Suppose you do the following: Open a Simulink model Select Tools > Polyspace > Polyspace for RTW Embedded Coder . Click Configure . The Project Manager perspective of the Polyspace verification environment opens.  You see that some options, for example, -allow-negative-operand-in-shift,  are specified. However, when you run the verification, you observe from the verification log that the software does not actually use these options.
Workaround: The following is a workaround:Open the Simulink model.Select Tools > Polyspace > Polyspace for RTW Embedded Coder.Click Configure. The Project Manager perspective of the Polyspace verification environmentinterface opens.De-select and re-select each option that you want.Save the configuration file.When you rerun the verification, you see that the software now applies the options in the verification.

Summary: Incorrect green NIV check or UNR check for some volatile global variables that point to structures
Description: Consider the following code. volatile int alea ;struct S1 { char x ; };struct S2 { int x2 ; int y2 ; };volatile struct S1 s1;void foo(void) { struct S2 s2; if (alea) {  s2.x2 = 0 ;  s2.y2 = 0 ; } if (alea) {  s1.x=1;  {   char *p = &s2 + alea ;   int res = *p ; /* green NIV that must be orange */  } } assert(s1.x==1);} In a verification, the software may generate an incorrect green NIV check for *p  because the global variable s1 , an instance of the structure S1 , is volatile. Consider the following code, which is slightly different from the previous example. struct S1 { char* x1 ;} ;struct S2 { int x2 ; int y2 ;} ;volatile struct S1 s1 ;volatile int alea ;void foo(void) { struct S2 s2 = {1, 1} ; if (alea) { s2.x2 = 0 ; s2.y2 = 0 ;} if (alea) {  s1.x1= 0 ;   {    char **p = (char*) (&s2 + alea) ;    if (*p != 0) { /* incorrect gray UNR */     *p = 1 ;   }  } } assert(s1.x1 != 0);} In this case, the software generates an incorrect gray UNR check because because the global variable s1 , an instance of the structure S1 , is volatile. You see these issues only in R2011a.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your Polyspace installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your Polyspace installation. By default this is in c:\PolySpace or in /usr/local/PolySpace on Linux systems. This folder is referred to as <POLYSPACE> in subsequent steps. PolySpace_Common and PolySpaceFor<lang>_R20xx are the two principal sub-folders of <POLYSPACE>.Exit any open Polyspace Graphical Interface and stop Polyspace queue manager service.Download the attached _[1|2].zip files associated with this Bug Report and store the files in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the *.zip files. To see the contents of a .zip file: Windows Systems: Open each .zip file and look at the contents.Linux Systems: Type the following for each zip in a shell:unzip -l <POLYSPACE>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the two .zip files. Windows Systems: Open the two .zip files.Extract the contents of the files to your <POLYSPACE> folder.Linux Systems: Type the following in a shell for each zip file: cd <POLYSPACE>unzip <.zip file>Check that binaries have appropriate ¡®read & execute¡¯ permissions.Delete the .zip files in the <POLYSPACE> folder.Restart Polyspace queue manager service.?

Summary: Model extracted from atomic subsystem by sldvextract fails to update
Description: If an atomic subsystem includes signals with names resolved to Simulink signal objects, and those objects are not resolved to Data Store Read or Data Store Write blocks inside the subsystem, then the model extracted from that subsystem by sldvextract  fails to update.

Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Change log:July 19, 2011: attachment_747207_10b_2011-07-19.zip: initial version, works on R2010b and R2010bSP1 onlyJuly 19, 2011: attachment_747207_11a_2011-07-19.zip: initial version, works on R2011 only

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: For model with continuous states and non-inlined multi-rate subsystems
Description: The simulation and code generation results might not match if all of the following conditions are true: A model contains continuous states and uses a fixed-step continuous solver. The model contains a multi-rate subsystem block, which is atomic and the Function packaging  parameter is set to Function . The multi-rate subsystem must contain the fastest discrete rate but no continuous time blocks. The multi-rate subsystem must contain a multi-rate subsystem of the same kind where the Function packaging  parameter is set to Function .  In the generated code, the multi-rate subsystem's output function with the fastest discrete rate might not be called.

Summary: Gain block might fail to report overflow, or might report overflow when saturation occurs
Description: Suppose that your model contains a Gain block with the following characteristics: Input signal data type, "Output data type", and "Parameter data type" are the same. Input signal data type, "Output data type", and "Parameter data type" are floating point or any built-in integer type.  Then: If you select "Saturate on integer overflow" on the Gain block and overflow occurs during simulation, the block does not report the overflow. You do not see an error or warning, even if "Detect overflow" in the Signals group on the Diagnostics > Data Validity pane of the Model Configuration Parameters is set to "error" or "warning". If you select "Saturate on integer overflow" on the Gain block and overflow occurs during simulation, the resulting error or warning message reports saturation instead of reporting overflow.  ?
Workaround: None.

Summary: meshgrid on the GPU can return incorrect results when one of the first two input arguments (but not both) is a scalar
Description: When any input is a GPUArray, this statment returns at least one incorrect output if either a  or b  is a scalar, but not both: [A, B, C] = meshgrid(a, b, c) This problem also occurs when using only two inputs.
Workaround: You can work around this problem by using ndgrid instead of meshgrid. as shown in the following code, where a is a scalar:a = 1;b = gpuArray(1:5);c = 1:2;[A, B, C] = ndgrid(a, b, c);A = gather(A); B = gather(B); C = gather(C);reorder = [2, 1, 3];A = permute(A, reorder);B = permute(B, reorder);C = permute(C, reorder);A = gpuArray(A);B = gpuArray(B);C = gpuArray(C);If you are calling meshgrid with only two input arguments, the workaround is far simpler. Because the outputs A and B are 2-D arrays, you can use transpose instead of permute to manipulate the results. Since transpose is implemented on the GPU, this workaround requires no data transfer between the GPU and the CPU:[A, B] = ndgrid(a, b);A = transpose(A);B = transpose(B);

Summary: Incorrect gray UNR check when using Boolean shortcut operator and then
Description: The software may generate an incorrect gray UNR check when the Boolean shortcut operator and then  is used as the condition in an if  statement. For example, consider the following code. if A and then B and then C then first_instruction -- UNR check ...end if;  The software may generate an incorrect gray UNR check for first_instruction  inside the if  statement body.

Description: It is possible for HDF5 applications to create integer datatypes with a size that does not match up with MATLAB's integer datatype sizes (1, 2, 4, or 8 bytes).  These datatypes cause H5INFO to error out and not return any metadata.

Summary: Wrong message associated with UNR check on if-condition instruction
Description: On the following code, Polyspace for C++ displays the wrong if-condition  message, even when semantic has been respected. The message says that the if-condition is always true, which is the reverse of the expected message - the if-condition is always false when using a 32-Bit target like i386 : int main () {  int volatile a = 1; if (sizeof(int) != sizeof(long)) { // wrong if-condition message on UNR check   assert(0) ;   return 5 ; }  assert(1) ; return a; }  This happens only when using Polyspace for C++ under specific conditions. For example, when a 'return ' is in the if  statement.

Summary: Specifying RTWData for inactive variant may cause segmentation violation
Description: If you specify the RTWData  parameter for a block that is part of an inactive variant, a segmentation violation may occur when you generate code for the model.
Workaround: If you intend to generate code for a model, do not specify the RTWData parameter for blocks that are inside inactive variants in the model.

Summary: Memory reduction generates errors or halts training too early
Description: Training with memory reduction on time series data generates an error. Training on static data with TRAINLM  results in the training stopping too early. For example, the following code can generate the problem: [x,t] = house_dataset;net = feedforwardnet;net.efficiency.memoryReduction = 10;net = train(net,x,t);
Workaround: To work around this problem, place the content from the zip attachment into the current working directory.

Summary: waverec3 uses the incorrect subband coefficients for reconstruction
Description: In a subset of the 8 possible subband filtering operations in the 3-D discrete wavelet transform, waverec3  uses the incorrect subband coefficients in the reconstruction. For example X = reshape(1:64,4,4,4);  dwtOut = dwt3(X,'haar');  HLL1 = idwt3(dwtOut,'HLL');   correctly reconstructs the 3-D input based on the 'HLL' subband. Using waverec3 , wdec = wavedec3(X,1,'haar'); HLL2 = waverec3(wdec,'HLL');   incorrectly reconstructs the 3-D input based on the 'HLL' subband. In this case, waverec3  actually reconstructs the input based on the 'LLH' subband. ? ? ? ? ? ?

Summary: In Rapid Accelerator simulation mode, the simulation of models that have multiple external input ports when loaded with signals of structure format causes the last signal to to be loaded to every input port
Description: The simulation of a model incorrectly causes the last signal to be loaded to every input port when the following is true: The model is configured for Rapid Accelerator mode. The model has multiple external input ports that are loaded with signals of structure  format.  Note that this is not an issue for the signal format matrix .

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: MATLAB Coder (formerly Embedded MATLAB) generates incorrect code with dynamic memory allocation enabled
Description: This bug occurs only when: MATLAB Coder (formerly Embedded MATLAB) generates a MEX function for a matrix of structures that contain no data Dynamic memory allocation is enabled  For example, the following MATLAB function triggers the bug: function s = foo(n) %#codegen (formerly %#eml)assert(n < 10);t.f1 = zeros(1, 0, 'int32');t.f2 = zeros(0, 1, 'int32');s.y1 = repmat(t,1,n);s.y2 = repmat(t,2,n);
Workaround: Remove structure fields that do not contain any data. The compilation report can be used to find such fields as follows:Enable the code generation report. (For example, at the command line, use the codegen (formerly emlc) -report option.Generate a MEX function.Click the "View report" link to open the code generation report.In the report, click the "Variables" tab.Click the '+' symbol next to each structure variable to expand it.On the "Variables" tab, click on the "Size" column to sort the variables and fields by size.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect variable access in generated code for inlined variant subsystem
Description: Due to a potential error in the generated code for an inlined variant system, a situation can arise in which a variable of the same name is declared locally in both an Outputs  code block and an Update  code block, assigned a value in the Outputs  code, and then referenced in the Update  code as if it had a value. For example, consider the following annotated excerpt of the generated code for an inlined variant subsystem. Notice that: In the scope of the Outputs  code, rtb_Switch  is declared as a local variable and assigned a value. In the scope of the Update  code, rtb_Switch  is declared again as a local variable, not assigned a value, and then its value is accessed as an input.  When compiled, code of this form might get a warning, but no error, and when executed, might behave differently than the corresponding model simulation. void mymodel_step(void){#if variant_sub1    /* Outputs */    {       /* local block i/o variable */       real_T rtb_Switch;       if (mymodel_rtU.In1) > 4.0) {          rtb_Switch = 3.0;       } else {          rtb_Switch = mymodel_rtU.In1;       }       mymodel_rtB.Merge = rtb_Switch;   }#endif     /* variant_sub1 */   mymodel_rtY.Out1 = mymodel_rtB.Merge;#if variant_sub1    /* Update */   {       /* local block i/o variable */       real_T rtb_Switch;       /* Update for UnitDelay: 'S1/Unit Delay2' */       mymodel_rtDWork.Var1.UnitDelay_DSTATE = rtb_Switch;   }#endif     /* variant_sub1 */
Workaround: To work around this bug, take one of the following actions:Locate the code pattern that produces the issue in the generated code for your inlined variant subsystem. First, identify a variable that is declared and assigned in the scope of one code block, and for which a variable of the same name is declared but not assigned prior to use in the scope of a second code block. Next, identify the block whose output is represented by that variable, and then Testpoint that block output.Open the block dialog box for the affected subsystem, go to the Code Generation tab, and reset the Function packaging parameter from Inline to Function or Reusable function.

Summary: In Rapid Accelerator simulation mode and Simulink Coder's Rapid Simulation target, simulation of models that have root input ports when loaded with framed-based signals of structure without time format causes the first frame to be skipped
Description: The simulation of a model incorrectly skips the first frame when the following is true: The model is configured for Rapid Accelerator simulation mode or Simulink Coder's Rapid Simulation target. The model has root input ports that are loaded with framed-based signals of format structure without time.  Note that this is not an issue with signal formats matrix  or structure with time .
Workaround: As a workaround, do one of the following:Change the structure without time format to structure with time by adding the time vector.Add one frame of data with dummy values at the beginning of the structure without time signal values.

Description: Running a model in Accelerator mode or generating model code might fail with a "path does not exist " error if the Simulink Coder (rtw) build process encounters and tries to analyze a path that contains a junction point with restricted access. This error is most likely to occur on a non-English Windows platform in which the Program Files  folder exists as both (a) the English variant of the name, such as C:\Program Files , and (b) a restricted access junction point (a form of symbolic link) to the folder with a localized translation of "Program Files" as its name. For example, if the localized program files path is C:\Programme , an Accelerator mode simulation or a model build might fail with a message similar to the following: The path 'C:\Programme\MATLAB\R2010bSP1' does not exist
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the win32 or win64 version of the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file, open the .zip file and look at the contents.Extract the files from the .zip file.Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:If you see ¡°Error: Access is denied¡± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start WinZip in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting Run as administrator.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: Incorrect parameters set for Bus Speed and bit timing while using Vector hardware.
Description: The CAN Configuration block can set incorrect Bus Speed and other bit timing parameters (SJW, TSEG1, TSEG2 and NumSamples) when you use Vector Hardware. If you change the default bus speed value (500000), the CAN Configuration block will not set the correct values when it connects to the channel on the device.
Workaround: If you see this behavior in R2011a or earlier versions, unzip and install the attached file. Summary: solve fails on expressions with independent variable I
Description: When you try to solve an equation with respect to the symbolic variable I , the solve  command fails:  syms Isolve(I + 1, I)ans =Warning: The result cannot be displayed due a previously interruptedcomputation or out of memory. Run 'reset(symengine)' and rerun the commandsto regenerate the result.> In sym.disp at 22  In sym.display at 17
Workaround: Avoid using I as a symbolic variable.

Summary: Variable-precision numbers can be displayed with wrong exponent
Description: In rare circumstances, variable-precision numbers are displayed with wrong exponent, such as 1.01*10^244 instead of 1.01*10^(-24). This does not affect any computations, except conversions to double-precision numbers.
Workaround: Disable truncation of trailing zeroes in the mantissa:evalin(symengine, 'Pref::trailingZeroes(TRUE)');To make this setting permanent, edit matlab/toolbox/symbolic/mupad/share/lib/sysinit.mu and add the following line at the end:Pref::trailingZeroes(TRUE):

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Possible wrong answer from Discrete-Time Integrator block with ground non-scalar reset signal
Description: The generated code of a Discrete-Time Integrator block, with an external reset signal that is connected to a non-scalar Ground block, might produce a wrong answer.
Workaround: Do one of the following:Change the ground reset signal from non-scalar to scalar.Replace the Ground block with a Constant block.

Summary: Simulation And Code Generation Mismatch: For multiplication of a non-binary fixed-point by a constant 1 of integer type.
Description: The generated code computes wrong answers for multiplications by 1 involving certain fixed-point types. This might occur for an internal multiplication operation with the following properties: Input 1 is a fixed-point variable with a non-binary fixed-point data type. For example, the data type is sfix32_S1p5en5 .  Input 2 is a constant containing the number 1 . The type of the output is an integer type, such as int16 .  A wrong answer might occur by any block introducing multiplication operations as part of its internal algorithm, such as a Product block or a Gain block. However, a wrong answer might not happen in all cases. It is dependent on the specific data types and the slope of the non-binary fixed-point data type. If all of the data types on the multiplication operations are the same, a wrong answer does not occur.
Workaround: To work around this problem, do one of the following:Use one or more Data Type Conversion blocks or adjust the block's output type to avoid data type combinations that trigger the problem. For example, use binary fixed-point types or a homogeneously typed operation.Change the constant 1 so that it is not a constant by doing one of the following:Make a parameter tunable.On the Optimization pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog, clear the Inline parameters checkbox.Specify the constant signal as a global variable. Open the Signal Properties dialog and on the Code Generation  tab, specify a Storage class for the constant signal.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: y = filter(b,1,x) might return wrong answers for multi-channel inputs
Description: The filter  function might return wrong answers for multi-channel inputs when called in the form: y = filter(b,1,x)  or y = filter(b,a,x)  where a  is any non-zero scalar. The first column of y  is correct, but subsequent columns might not be correct. This error occurs only if all   of the following conditions are met: x  is not a vector. The second input argument to filter  is a scalar. 2*length(b) <= size(x,1) <= 22+length(b)   filter  is called with only three inputs. filter  is called with only one output.  For example, if b  has 7 elements, the error will not occur if x  has 30 or more rows.
Workaround: Use[y,~] = filter(b,1,x)ory = filter(b,1,x,[],1)

Summary: Simulating models with Physical Modeling product blocks and Subsystem Variant blocks may crash MATLAB
Description: Models that contain certain combinations of blocks from Physical Modeling products (for example, SimMechanics or Simscape)  and Subsystem Variant blocks may cause MATLAB to crash during simulation.
Workaround: If your model does not use Mux blocks to create virtual bus signals, consider setting the Configuration Parameter > Diagnostics > Connectivity > Mux blocks used to create bus signals parameter to error. See Bug Report 250660 for more information about upgrading your model to be compliant with this diagnostic.

Summary: The sqp algorithm of fmincon can return incorrect Lagrange multipliers in the lambda structure when the initial point is a solution.
Description: Under certain circumstances, the sqp  algorithm of fmincon  can return incorrect Lagrange multipliers in the lambda  structure. This happens when: The fmincon algorithm is 'sqp'  The initial point x0  is a solution (the gradient of the objective function is zero) There are no equality constraints (Aeq = [] , ceq = [] ) All bound and inequality constraints are strictly satisfied at x0  (A*x0 < b , c(x0) < 0 )  Because the initial point is a solution with no active constraints, the optimal Lagrange multipliers are all zero. However, when fmincon  stops after one iteration, it returns spurious Lagrange multipliers of all ones in the lambda  structure. In this case, fmincon  also returns exit flag 2 .
Workaround: To ensure that the lambda structure is correct when fmincon stops after one iteration, try another fmincon algorithm. Set the Algorithm option to 'interior-point' or 'active-set' .

Summary: "What's This?" context help doesn't work in Configuration Parameters for C166 and MPC5xx processors
Description: "What's This?" context help doesn't work in the C166 Options  and ET MPC5xx...  panes located in the Configuration Parameters  dialog box under Code Generation . The C166 Options  and ET MPC5xx...  panes are only visible if you set the System target file  parameter to one of the following files: c166.tlc  c166_grt.tlc  mpc555exp.tlc  mpc555pil.tlc  mpc555rt.tlc  mpc555rt_grt.tlc
Workaround: Search the product help for the parameter name instead of using the "What's This?" context help. Open the product help by selecting the Help > Product Help menu items. When the product help appears, enter the parameter name in the search text box and press Enter on your keyboard.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Mapping boolean_T data type to a data type other than uint8_T might affect the numerical results of the generated code
Description: On the Data Type Replacement  pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, when boolean_T  is mapped to an underlying type such as, int8_T , int16_T , uint16_T , uint32_T  or int32_T , the generated code might produce a numerically different result compared to the simulation result. For example, mapping boolean_T  to a custom data type name whose underlying type is int8_T  can change the initial value of boolean trigger signals used with edge triggered subsystems int8_T  can change the generated code of the Weighted Sample Time block whose output signal is a boolean data type int32_T  can change the generated code of the Combinatorial Logic block's computed parameter values.
Workaround: On the Data Type Replacement pane, rename the data type for boolean_T only if the underlying type is consistent with the default of uint8_T.

Summary: Simulink Design Verifier derives incorrect ranges for fixed-point data type signals in Design Error Detection analysis
Description: In the model's Configuration Parameters dialog box under the Optimization > Simulation and code generation  section, if the option "Use integer division to handle net slopes that are reciprocals of integers" is selected, Simulink Design Verifier derives incorrect ranges for fixed-point data type signals in Design Error Detection analysis.

Summary: commscope.eyediagram object does not update eye diagram measurements when you modify BERThreshold and EyeLevelBoundary properties
Description: The commscope.eyediagram object captures the eye diagram of an input signal and performs eye diagram measurements, such as eye opening, on the data. The BERThreshold and EyeLevelBoundary properties are used to setup the measurement conditions. These properties only effect how the object calculates measurement values and do not effect the way it collects the data. Changing these properties should update the jitter and eye opening measurements; however, the measurements are not updated.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems*: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh): cd <MATLAB>unzip <.zip file>Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.*Notes for Windows Systems:If you see ¡°Error: Access is denied¡± when attempting to extract the .zip file, you may need to start ¡°WinZip¡± in administrator mode. Do this by navigating to WinZip.exe or the WinZip Start menu entry, right-clicking on it, and selecting ¡°Run as administrator¡±.From within WinZip, find and open the downloaded .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect code generated when AUTOSAR input accesses both data and error status in QueuedExplicitReceive mode
Description: Real Time Workshop Embedded Coder can generate incorrect code for a model when you do all of the following: Set the system target file for the model to autosar.tlc . Access both the data and error status for an AUTOSAR application input Specify Data Access Mode  to be QueuedExplicitReceive  Connect the top model inport representing either the data or error status to the inport of a Switch  block  The code can be incorrect in one of the following ways: The RTE call that retrieves the data and error status can be invoked multiple times. The generated code can reference undeclared local variables, and fail to compile.
Workaround: The workaround is as follows:Group the contents of the top level of the model into a new subsystem. You do this by selecting the blocks and lines using a bounding box, and then selecting Edit >Create Subsystem.Select the new subsystem block.Right-click and select Subsystem Parameters to open the Function Block Parameters dialog box for the the subsystem.Select the Treat as atomic unit check box.From the drop-down list for Real-Time Workshop system code, select either Function or Reusable function.Click OK.?

Summary: Within a class folder, pcode *.m does not put P-coded files in the correct destination
Description: If the current folder name is of the form @className , then pcode *.m  creates P-coded files in the parent folder instead of the class folder where expected.
Workaround: Use the syntax pcode * instead of pcode *.m.

Summary: Generated code fails to compile due to unknown UNUSED_PARAMETER macro
Description: The UNUSED_PARAMETER  macro is defined in the model_types.h  header file, which is not visible to the shared utilities folder. When the generated code for the MATLAB Function block (formerly Embedded MATLAB Function block) is placed in the shared utilities folder and uses the UNUSED_PARAMETER  macro (which eliminates compiler warnings), the code fails to compile due to the unknown UNUSED_PARAMETER  macro.
Workaround: If UNUSED_PARAMETER is used in a file in the build folder, do not use shared utilities.

Summary: Supported version number of BullseyeCoverage is not specified and using incorrect version may cause errors
Description: Embedded Coder supports the use of the BullseyeCoverage tool for collecting code coverage metrics during a software-in-the-loop (SIL) or processor-in-the-loop (PIL) simulation. However, Embedded Coder is tested only with the following versions of the BullseyeCoverage tool: R2011b - 8.0.19 (Linux) R2011b - 8.0.20 (Windows) R2011a - 7.14.20 R2010b - 7.13.14 R2010a - 7.13.14  Releases R2010a, R2010b, R2011a and R2011b are incompatible with version 8.1.1 and later of BullseyeCoverage. If you are using an incompatible version, you may see the error The column number for the first annotation must be zero . ?

Summary: Segmentation violation during AUTOSAR code generation for subsystem using Simulink.Signal object with non-auto data type
Description: Exporting functions from an AUTOSAR multi-runnable component might result in a segmentation violation if both of the following conditions exist: In the model, a signal representing an interrunnable variable resolves to a Simulink.Signal  object. (The error does not occur if the signal is located inside an exported runnable rather than between two runnables.) The Data type  value for the Simulink.Signal  object is set to a value other than auto .
Workaround: To work around this bug, take one of the following actions:Set the Data type value for the Simulink.Signal object to auto.Modify the model so that signals connecting runnables do not resolve to a Simulink.Signal object. Potentially, you could modify signals inside exported runnables to resolve to the Simulink.Signal object.

Summary: GUIDE: Error when opening GUIs with ActiveX controls in MATLAB? R2011a
Description: In MATLAB R2011a, when you open a GUIDE GUI with ActiveX controls, the following Java exception might display in the Command Window. As a result, you cannot open the GUI in GUIDE for editing.  java.lang.ClassCastException: [D cannot be cast to [Ljava.lang.String;at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.LayoutEditor.completed(LayoutEditor.java:2207)at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.LayoutEditor$ReadFigureCompleted.completed(LayoutEditor.java:3020)at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.utils.LayoutWorker.runOnMatlabThread(LayoutWorker.java:55)at com.mathworks.jmi.MatlabWorker$2.run(MatlabWorker.java:79)at com.mathworks.jmi.MatlabWorker.start(MatlabWorker.java:248)at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.utils.LayoutWorker.start(LayoutWorker.java:70)at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.utils.LayoutWorker.fevalConsoleOutput(LayoutWorker.java:41)at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.LayoutLooper.readFigure(LayoutLooper.java:712)at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.LayoutEditor.openLayoutEditor(LayoutEditor.java:2004)at com.mathworks.toolbox.matlab.guide.LayoutEditor.openLayoutEditor(LayoutEditor.java:1967)
Workaround: Replace guidefunc.m in MATLABROOT\toolbox\matlab\guide with the attached guidefunc.m file. MATLABROOT is the folder returned when you type matlabroot in the Command Window.

Description: When a model reads digital values from ports 25 to 48 of a Measurement Computing board, the values from ports 1 to 24 are returned instead. Similarly, when the model writes values to ports 25 to 48, the values are written to ports 1 to 24 instead. This bug affects the following boards: PCI-DDA02/12PCI-DDA02/16PCI-DDA04/12PCI-DDA04/16PCI-DDA08/12PCI-DDA08/16 ?

Summary: Segmentation violation when the Maximum or Minimum block's ROI port has a different sample time than the data input port
Description: A segmentation violation occurs when the following two conditions are met: Your Simulink model contains a Maximum or Minimum block that is in ROI Processing mode. The input signal at the ROI input port of the Maximum or Minimum block has a different sample time than the input signal at the data input port.
Workaround: To workaround this issue, make sure that the input to the block's ROI port has the same sample time as the input to the data input port. If you set the sample time of the ROI input signal to "-1", the signal will inherit its sample time from the input signal at the data input port.

Summary: MATLAB can crash producing a log with "Transform::computeTransform"
Description: While using MATLAB Graphics, MATLAB can exit abruptly generating a crash log that contains the words "Transform::computeTransform". The specific steps that lead to this crash are not yet fully known, but they involve the use of MATLAB Graphics. If you were not using MATLAB Graphics at the time of the crash, then please contact Technical Support for help troubleshooting:  https://www.mathworks.com/support/contact_us.html

Summary: Using the error function in finish.m to report shutdown problems crashes MATLAB
Description: As part of its shutdown processing, MATLAB runs the user supplied function finish.m . Normally, the user can use the error  function in finish.m  to report an error. The error  function should print the supplied error message to the desktop and return control to the command line. In 2011a, calling the error  function from finish.m  will crash MATLAB.
Workaround: The call to the error function can be emulated using fprintf to report the error message and then canceling the exit request. For example, the error call: error 'Shutdown has failed";can be replaced by: fprintf(2, 'Shutdown has failed\n"); quit cancel;

Summary: MATLAB or components deployed from MATLAB will not start on Windows platforms with 32 or more logical CPUs
Description: On workstations with 32 or more logical CPUs, MATLAB starts up and displays the expected windows, but does not display the MATLAB command prompt. This bug also affects the MATLAB Compiler Runtime (MCR), such that components deployed from MATLAB Compiler or Builder products, e.g., standalone executables, shared libraries, add-ins, will hang on startup.
Workaround: Workaround for R2011a releaseInstallation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation.Locate your MATLAB installation (typically C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2011a for this release on Windows). Use this value for the term <MATLAB> folder in the following steps.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store on your desktop.For 32-bit Windows, select attachment_732450_11a_win32_2011-05-16.zipFor 64-bit Windows, select attachment_732450_11a_win64_2011-05-16.zipMake a backup copy of your <MATLAB>\bin\win32\libutil.dll and/or <MATLAB>\bin\win64\libut.dll files.Extract the contents of the file to a new folder your desktop.Move the extracted files to your <MATLAB> folder in Explorer.Delete the .zip file from your desktop.Workaround for earlier releasesUse the start Windows command to force MATLAB or the deployed component to run on 31 cores or less. To do this:Open a Windows command terminal, also known as a DOS prompt, by typing cmd in the start menu run field.Start MATLAB or the deployed component with the following command: start /affinity 7FFFFFFF applicationwhere application is the path to the MATLAB executable or the deployed component executable (note that double quotes are necessary if the path has spaces), such as start /affinity 7FFFFFFF "C:\Program Files\MATLAB\R2010b\bin\win64\matlab.exe"To make future startups easier, it is possible to put the above command in a Windows batch file and simply run the batch file.Change log:May 16, 2011: attachment_732450_11a_[win32|win64]_2011-05-16.zip: Initial version.

Summary: Compilation error for models containing signal objects of struct data type
Description: If your model contains a bus signal whose data type is set to struct , you may see an unclear error message when you compile the model.

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Code generation can fail when you use the Selector block with VNT blocks
Description: When you use VNT blocks and use Selector  block from Simulink and generate code, you will see this undeclared datatype CAN_MESSAGE error: ### Compiling CAN2_ert.ccl -c -DCRTAPI1=_cdecl -DCRTAPI2=_cdecl -nologo -GS -D_X86_=1 -DWIN32 -D_WIN32 -W3 -D_WINNT -D_WIN32_WINNT=0x0500 -DNTDDI_VERSION=0x05000000 -D_WIN32_IE=0x0500 -DWINVER=0x0500 -D_MT -MT /wd4996 /fp:precise /Od /Oy- -DMODEL=CAN2_ert -DNUMST=4 -DNCSTATES=0 -DMAT_FILE=0 -DINTEGER_CODE=0 -DONESTEPFCN=1 -DTERMFCN=1 -DHAVESTDIO -DMULTI_INSTANCE_CODE=0 -DMT=0 CAN2_ert.c CAN2_ert.cCAN2_ert.c(74) : error C2065: 'CAN_MESSAGE' : undeclared identifierCAN2_ert.c(74) : error C2146: syntax error : missing ';' before identifier 'tmp'CAN2_ert.c(74) : error C2065: 'tmp' : undeclared identifierCAN2_ert.c(74) : error C2109: subscript requires array or pointer type
Workaround: Install the attached zip file to work around this issue.Log in to an account with permissions to overwrite files in the MATLAB installation directory.Locate your MATLAB installation directory by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB Command window.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file and store the file in the installation directory.Open the zip file note the contents.Create a backup for each of the files listed in the zip file.Extract the files to your installation directory.Delete the downloaded .zip file.In case you encounter a Windows System Access denied error when you attempt to extract the zip file:Start WinZip in administrator mode.From WinZip, find and open the downloaded zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your installation directory.

Summary: The values stored in the PathGains property of the MIMO fading channel object returned by the mimochan function does not match the gain values used by the filter method.
Description: mimochan function returns a MIMO fading channel object. This object has a property called PathGains that stores the current path gain values for the MIMO channel. The value of the PathGains property does not match the values used in the filter method. See the following example for more details. Example: chan = mimochan(1, 2, 1, 0, [0 1 2]); x = [1 0 0].'; y = filter(chan,x); pg = chan.PathGains; Here we set the delays at multiples of the sampling period, the time response of the filter should be the same as the values stored at the PathGains property, but we get those values on the wrong positions (compare y with pg).

Summary: Terminate function of a function-call subsystem called multiple times
Description: If a function-call subsystem has wide function call input (multiple callers) and the function calls are initiated by the Function-Call Generator block, then the generated code might call the terminate function multiple times.

Summary: competlayer networks assign all vectors to one class
Description: competlayer  networks assign all input vectors to one class after training. For instance, after training a network and testing it as follows, the first row of y  is all 1's, with 0's in the other five rows. When working properly, y  should have 1's distributed between the six rows, representing samples assigned to all six classes.  x = iris_dataset; net = competlayer(6); net = train(net,x); y = net(x); classes = vec2ind(y);
Workaround: To work around this problem, place the files included in the attached zip file into the current workspace folder.

Summary: Sporadic wrong results from snapshot linearization of Simscape models
Description: Linearization of Simscape models using time-based snapshots sometimes gives wrong results for the (A,B,C,D) matrices placed in the workspace. This issue applies to both the Simulink Timed-Based Linearization block and the Simulink Control Design snapshot linearization. With the Timed-Based Linearization block, the linearization results might be incorrect for any model containing Simscape blocks. With Simulink Control Design, the situation is more complex because you can obtain linearization snapshots in two ways: Obtain operating points at the snapshots and then linearize after simulating. This way produces correct results. Obtain operating points and linearize immediately as the simulation is running. This way can produce wrong results.Specifically, if you select Tools  > Control Design  > Linear Analysis  to open the Control and Estimation Tools Manager, you see two nodes under Project , called Operating Points  and Linearization Task . Selecting Linearization Task , then selecting the Operating Points  tab and simulation snapshots  to compute operating points produces wrong linearization results. This is the linearize-while-simulating method.This method corresponds to entering linearize('sys', SNAPSHOTTIMES)  at the command line, which you should also avoid.  Simulating using the Compute operating points  tab that appears if you select the Operating Point  node under Project  does not produce any known errors in the linearization results.
Workaround: With Simulink Control Design:Select the Operating Points node under Project. Select simulation snapshots in the drop-down list to the right of Compute operating points using.Click the Compute operating points button.Open the Linearization Task node and make sure that Existing operating points is selected as the operating point type.Select the operating points you just computed. Click the Linearize button.The equivalent command-line workaround is to use findop to obtain snapshot operating points and pass these operating points to the linearize command.With the Simulink Timed-Based Linearization block:After you note the chosen snapshot times, remove the Timed-Based Linearization block from the model.In the model Configuration Parameters dialog box, under the Data Import/Export node:a. Make sure to save the model states to the workspace, by selecting the States check box.b. Make sure that, under Save Options, you select Produce additional output in the Output options drop-down list. For the additional output times, enter your chosen snapshot times.From the command line, use dlinmod once for each snapshot point to linearize at that snapshot point. In the dlinmod function, pass the states, top-level inputs, and linearization time as arguments.

Summary: Generating code multiple times for a model containing function-call Model blocks may produce different code, even when the model is not changing
Description: If a model contains multiple function-call Model blocks, then each time that you generate code for that model, the generated code may change the order of calls to certain functions in the referenced model for consecutive builds of the model. This can occur even when the model is not changing. The numerical results from the generated code are correct, even though the code has changed. For example, one portion of the generated code for one build may be: /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/Asynchronous Counter' */   mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/500ms Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/1 sec Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  On a consecutive build, without changing the model, the code may change to the following. Note that now the blocks are called in a different order.  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/500ms Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.u0msCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/1 sec Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.secCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));  /* Model Initialize fcn for ModelReference Block: '<Root>/Asynchronous Counter' */  mr_sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_cntr_initialize(1, rtmGetErrorStatusPointer  (sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_M),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtm),  &(sldemo_mdlref_fcncall_DWork.AsynchronousCounter_DWORK1.rtdw));

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Data consistency issues might occur when using the Minimize algebraic loop occurrences option on subsystem or model blocks
Description: Data consistency issues might occur when executing generated code if all of the following conditions are true: In your model, a multirate atomic subsystem or model block has a continuous sample time and one or more discrete subrate sample times. The block dialog option Minimize algebraic loop occurrences  is selected on the subsystem or model block. The subsystem or model block is involved in an artificial algebraic loop (AAL), which is being eliminated by Simulink. The model configuration parameter Tasking mode for periodic sample times  is set to Auto  or MultiTasking . The rate-based entry points of the generated code are executed in a preemptive multitasking environment.  Under these conditions, the persistent data associated with the discrete sample time blocks (for example, the DWork or BlockIO data) might be accessed from both the continuous (that is, base rate) task and the subrate task without data access (mutual exclusion) protection. This can cause data corruption during code execution.
Workaround: To help determine if the potential for this issue exists in code generated for your model, you can do the following:Identify at least one multirate atomic subsystem or model block in your model with continuous sample time and one or more discrete subrate sample times.Clear the Minimize algebraic loop occurrences option on subsystems and model blocks in the model.On the Diagnostics > Solver pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, set the Algebraic loop parameter to error.Simulate the model to determine if any artificial algebraic loops are detected by Simulink.If an artificial algebraic loop is detected, then condition 3 listed above in the Description section is true.Modeling based workarounds for this issue include any of the following:Avoid using both continuous sample time and discrete subrate sample times in the atomic subsystem or model block.Convert the atomic subsystem to a virtual subsystem.Break the artificial algebraic loop by inserting a delay block.Run-time based workarounds for this issue include any of the following:Avoid executing the generated model entry points in a preemptive multitasking manner.On the Solver pane of the Configuration Parameters dialog box, set the value of the Tasking mode for periodic sample times parameter to SingleTasking.

Summary: Code from model with AUTOSAR array calibration parameter compiles with warnings and code from model with AUTOSAR structure calibration parameter does not compile
Description: Consider a model with an AUTOSAR calibration parameter. If the AUTOSAR calibration parameter is an array that feeds a reusable subsystem or is a parameter of a Look-Up Table (LUT) block, when you build the model, the software compiles the generated code but produces warnings. If the AUTOSAR calibration parameter is a structure calibration parameter that feeds a reusable subsystem or an Invoke AUTOSAR Server Operation  block, the software cannot compile the generated code and produces an error. A typical compilation error message follows: mcalprm_struct.c: In function 'Runnable_untitled_Step': mcalprm_struct.c:56: error: incompatible type for argument 1 of 'mcalprm_struct_Subsystem2' mcalprm_struct.c:103: error: incompatible type for argument 1 of 'Rte_Call_untitled_port_op'
Workaround: If the the AUTOSAR calibration parameter feeds, for example, a reusable subsystem or stateflow chart, then a workaround is to make the signal between the calibration parameter and the reusable subsystem a test point signal.There is no known workaround if the calibration parameter is a parameter of a LUT block.

Summary: TRAINBR uses test vectors for training
Description: Most backpropagation training functions split data into training, validation, and test sets. trainbr   uses only training and test sets. Vectors that would normally be assigned to a validation set are assigned to the training set. The problem is that test vectors were also being assigned to the training set.
Workaround: The workaround is to withhold test data from training. Then apply network to test data after training to determine test mean square error (for instance with MSE).

Summary: Analysis fails when alias signals feed Simulink Design Verifier blocks
Description: Simulink Design Verifier fails to analyze models that contain blocks from the Simulink Design Verifier library that have input signals of Simulink.NumericType  with IsAlias  parameters set to true. The software displays the following incompatibility message: Incompatible types. The input signal is of type '$AliasTypeName$' the specification is of type '$ActualDataTypeOfAliasType$'.
Workaround: Installation instructionsLog in to an account with appropriate permissions to overwrite files in your MATLAB installation. On Linux systems, this is typically the root account.Locate your MATLAB installation by typing matlabroot at the MATLAB command prompt. This folder is referred to as <MATLAB> in subsequent steps.Exit MATLAB.Download the attached .zip file associated with this Bug Report and store the file in the <MATLAB> folder.Make a backup copy of each of the files listed in the .zip file. To see the contents of the .zip file: Windows Systems: Open the .zip file and look at the contents.Linux/Macintosh Systems: Type the following in a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh):unzip -l <MATLAB>/<.zip file>Extract the files from the .zip file. Windows Systems: Open the .zip file.Extract the contents of the file to your <MATLAB> folder.Linux/Macintosh Systems: In a shell (Linux) or in a terminal window (Macintosh), type cd <MATLAB>.Unzip the .zip file.Delete the .zip file in the <MATLAB> folder.Change log.May 13, 2011: attachment_730055_9b_2011_05_13.zip: initial version, works on R2009b only

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Data store in If or Switch block condition expression might produce incorrect results
Description: You might get incorrect generated code when all of the following conditions are met in your model: A data store is used in the condition expression of an If or Switch block. At least one of the branches contains a Data Store Write block, which writes to the data store used in the If or Switch block condition expression. In the generated code, each branch of the if  or switch  statement contains one and only one for  loop and the they have identical start, end, and stride values.  The attached model "mDataStoreInCond.mdl" illustrates the issue.
Workaround: Use a separate Data Store Memory block in the branches of the If or Switch block.

Summary: When using the LCC compiler, loadlibrary does not find standard C headers.
Description: When using 32-bit MATLAB and the LCC compiler, the loadlibrary  command issues a warning similar to: Warning: Message from C preprocessor: lcc preprocessor error: MYHEADERNAME Could not find include file <stddef.h>  This is because the mex LCC configuration is expecting the system environment variable MATLAB  to be defined, but it is not properly defined on your system.
Workaround: Run the command setenv('matlab',matlabroot) before issuing a loadlibrary command with a header file as input.

Summary: Rate Transition block does not handle rate transition; Simulink errors instead
Description: In situations like the following, the Rate Transition block does not handle the rate transition; Simulink errors instead. If the following is true for block A: Does not specify a sample time (for example, the sample time is inherited or -1) Drives a Rate Transition block and other port-based sample time blocks (for example, other Rate Transition blocks)  Simulink might set the Rate Transition block input port sample time to match its output port sample time instead of the sample time assigned to block A. In this case, Simulink reports a rate transition diagnostic between block A and the Rate Transition block. This diagnostic message suggests placing a Rate Transition block between these two blocks. However, this recommendation does not resolve the issue.
Workaround: As a workaround, specify a sample time on the signal emanating from the block A output. To do this, do one of the following:Specify the sample time on block A or a block that drives block A.Insert a Signal Specification block on the signal and specify the sample time in the Signal Specification block.

Summary: Signal in a bus ouput from a latched Inport in a triggered subsystem can result in an invalid error
Description: Simulink may report an invalid error when all of these conditions exist: An Inport block in a triggered subsystem has Latch input by delaying outside signal  checked. The signal from this Inport is packaged into a bus. This bus operates on a bus-capable block such as Unit Delay or Bus Assignment.  The reported error is invalid and is similar to this: Selected signal 'XYZ' in 'model/Unit Delay' is not part of the bus entering the Bus Selector. One probable cause of this error is the use of Mux blocks instead of Bus Creator blocks to create buses in this model. Consider changing the 'Mux blocks used to create bus signals' diagnostic to 'error' in this model and replace any Muxes that are then reported with Bus Creators. Another possible cause of this error is the use of a bus-capable block (such as Merge or Unit Delay) that in this current situation is unable to propagate the bus downstream to the block reported in this error. Please see Simulink documentation for further information on composite (i.e. bus) signals and their proper usage.
Workaround: Uncheck Latch input by delaying outside signal on the Inport block.Insert a Signal Conversion block at the output of the Inport block. Configure the Output parameter as Contiguous copy or Bus copy, based on the signal. Also, check Exclude this block from 'Block reduction' optimization.Set the block priority of this Signal Conversion block to 0 so that Simulink sorts this block before other blocks. To do this, select the Signal Conversion block and execute the following command:      set_param(gcb,'Priority','0')

Summary: Incorrect Code Generation: Incorrect optimization of switch-case statement fall through causes wrong answers
Description: MATLAB Coder might  generate incorrect code for switch  statements that contain empty cases. Similarly, Simulink Coder might generate incorrect code for Switch Case blocks, Stateflow charts, and MATLAB Function blocks that contain switch  statements.  NOTE: This bug affects all Coder products. The following MATLAB example generates incorrect code: switch n    case 0    case 1        y = 1;    case 2    otherwise        y = 2;end In the generated code, the assignment y = 1  is missing.
Workaround: Use the following MATLAB commands to search the generated C code for possible occurrences of the bug:>> txt = fileread('generatedcode.c');>> m = regexp(txt,'case\s[^;]+case','match');The regexp command searches for a pair of case labels which are not separated by a semi-colon. If the generated code contains this pattern, then you should manually inspect the code to determine whether it is correct.In MATLAB Coder, the bug does not occur if every case statement contains a comment.No workarounds are currently known for incorrect code generated for Stateflow charts or for Switch Case blocks.
